one_line_takeaway: An unsupervised, label-free adversarial fine-tuning loss that keeps CLIP's perturbed features near its original clean features yields a drop-in robust vision encoder that makes LLaVA/OpenFlamingo resist imperceptible visual attacks while retaining far more clean performance than the prior supervised TeCoA baseline.
key_finding: FARE dominates the TeCoA baseline on the clean/robust trade-off. On LLaVA-1.5 (Table 1, average over COCO/Flickr30k/TextVQA/VQAv2), original CLIP scores 76.2 clean but is essentially non-robust (2.25 at 2/255, 1.0 at 4/255). FARE2 reaches 71.1 clean (+9.5 vs TeCoA2's 61.6) and 33.2 robust at 2/255 (+4.9); FARE4 reaches 65.0 clean (+9.8 vs TeCoA4's 55.2) and 26.3 robust at 4/255 (+2.3). On stealthy targeted attacks against LLaVA (Table 3, 6 target strings x 25 images), CLIP is broken 100% of the time at both radii, while at eps=2/255 all robust models break in 0% of cases; at eps=4/255 FARE4 and TeCoA4 stay fully robust (0%), and smaller-radius models break rarely (TeCoA2 3.3%, FARE2 2.0%). On zero-shot classification over ImageNet + 13 datasets (Table 4), clean average: CLIP 73.1, FARE2 67.0 (+7.0 vs TeCoA2 60.0), FARE4 61.1 (+6.9 vs TeCoA4 54.2); at eps=4/255 robust average FARE4 32.4 slightly beats TeCoA4 31.9 (CLIP 0.0). FARE also hallucinates less than TeCoA (POPE mean F1: CLIP 84.5, FARE2 80.8, TeCoA2 75.9, FARE4 76.3, TeCoA4 72.2) and reasons slightly better (SQA-I: CLIP 64.5, FARE2 63.4, FARE4 62.3 vs TeCoA2 61.1, TeCoA4 59.9). Robust encoders also block cross-model transfer attacks (Table 2), and reduce jailbreak success comparably to TeCoA (Table 7).
core_contribution: The paper proposes FARE (Fine-tuning for Adversarially Robust Embeddings), an unsupervised adversarial fine-tuning scheme for the CLIP vision encoder. Its own contribution is an embedding-based loss (Eq. 3): L_FARE = max_{||z-x||_inf <= eps} ||phi(z) - phi_Org(x)||_2^2, which forces the fine-tuned encoder's features on adversarially perturbed inputs to stay close to the ORIGINAL frozen CLIP features on clean inputs. This requires no labels and no text encoder, and because clean features are approximately preserved, the robust encoder can be dropped in as a replacement for CLIP inside large vision-language models (LLaVA, OpenFlamingo) and in zero-shot classifiers WITHOUT retraining or fine-tuning downstream components. The paper is explicit that the prior supervised method TeCoA (Mao et al., 2023) is the pre-existing BASELINE, not this work's contribution; FARE is presented as the improvement over it. The authors also prove Theorem 3.1, showing that keeping the L2 embedding distance small bounds the change in image-text cosine similarity, thus preserving zero-shot performance.
threat_model: L_inf-bounded adversarial perturbations on the vision (image) modality of CLIP-based LVLMs, primarily at eps=2/255 (imperceptible) and eps=4/255 (barely noticeable). The adversary is a malicious third party who distributes manipulated images to defraud users or spread misinformation. Attack settings covered: (i) untargeted performance-degrading attacks on captioning/VQA; (ii) stealthy targeted attacks that force an exact attacker-chosen output string (e.g., phishing URLs, disinformation) via strong white-box APGD; (iii) transfer/black-box attacks using a surrogate LVLM (relevant to closed-source models sharing the same CLIP encoder); and (iv) visual jailbreak attacks (Qi et al. 2023, up to eps=64/255). The defense is the robustified vision encoder; the paper explicitly notes it does not defend the language side and that its jailbreak evaluation is preliminary and may overestimate robustness.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) the work focuses only on CLIP-based LVLMs; other LVLM architectures are not evaluated. (2) Robustness is restricted to the visual input space; the language side of LVLMs is not defended. (3) The study does not examine robust-CLIP LVLMs for instruction following, explainability, or perception tasks. (4) The jailbreak evaluation relies on a single attack (Qi et al., 2023), which the authors explicitly call preliminary and warn may overestimate robustness. Additional observable trade-offs from the tables: FARE2 trades away robustness at the larger 4/255 test radius (e.g., zero-shot classification average robustness at 4/255 is 20.5 for FARE2, below TeCoA2's 27.0 and TeCoA4's 31.9), so no single FARE variant is best everywhere; and on the ImageNet source dataset TeCoA (supervised on ImageNet labels) still edges out FARE in both clean and robust accuracy.

quantitative_results: LLaVA-1.5 avg over 4 datasets (Table 1): clean CLIP 76.2, FARE2 71.1 (+9.5 vs TeCoA2 61.6), FARE4 65.0 (+9.8 vs TeCoA4 55.2); robust @2/255 FARE2 33.2 (+4.9), @4/255 FARE4 26.3 (+2.3)
OpenFlamingo-9B avg (Table 1): clean CLIP 53.0, FARE2 51.4 (+5.0 vs TeCoA2 46.4), FARE4 47.5 (+5.6 vs TeCoA4 41.9)
Stealthy targeted attacks on LLaVA (Table 3): CLIP 100% success at both 2/255 and 4/255; at 2/255 all robust models 0%; at 4/255 TeCoA2 3.3%, FARE2 2.0%, TeCoA4 0%, FARE4 0%

Zero-shot classification avg over 14 datasets (Table 4): clean CLIP 73.1, FARE2 67.0 (+7.0 vs TeCoA2 60.0), FARE4 61.1 (+6.9 vs TeCoA4 54.2); robust @2/255 FARE4 45.9 vs TeCoA4 42.3, CLIP 0.0; robust @4/255 FARE4 32.4 vs TeCoA4 31.9, CLIP 0.0
POPE hallucination mean F1 (Table 5): CLIP 84.5, FARE2 80.8, TeCoA2 75.9, FARE4 76.3, TeCoA4 72.2
SQA-I accuracy (Table 6): CLIP 64.5, FARE2 63.4 (+2.3 vs TeCoA2 61.1), FARE4 62.3 (+2.4 vs TeCoA4 59.9)
Transfer attacks (Table 2): adversarial COCO images (eps=4/255) drop CLIP-based targets to ~1-8 CIDEr, but robust encoders retain ~65-116 CIDEr, blocking transfer
Jailbreak (Table 7, eps=64/255, 'any' category): CLIP 36/40 harmful, TeCoA4 23/40, FARE4 23/40
Training cost: 2 epochs on ImageNet, ~0.2% of original CLIP training compute (which was 32 epochs over 400M images)
baselines_compared: TeCoA (text-guided contrastive adversarial training, supervised, Mao et al., 2023) at eps=2/255 (TeCoA2) and eps=4/255 (TeCoA4)
